subtopics.name: Ancient Olympics (776 BCE – 393 CE)
content: Originated in Olympia, Greece
Held every 4 years (Olympiad) in honour of Greek God Zeus
Only men could compete; women could not even attend
Events: Running, Wrestling, Chariot Racing, Javelin, Long Jump, Pankration, Discus
Wrestling first included in Ancient Olympics: 708 BCE #imp
Games banned by Roman Emperor Theodosius I in 393 CE – called 'pagan'

subtopics.name: Revival & Modern Olympics
content: Modern Olympics revived by Pierre de Coubertin (French educator)
IOC (International Olympic Committee) founded in 1894, Paris
Aim: Promote friendship, peace and education through sport
First Modern Olympics: 1896, Athens, Greece
Participants: 14 nations, 241 athletes – only men
Women first included: 1900 Paris Olympics
Games held every 4 years, except 1916, 1940, 1944 (World Wars I & II)
Tokyo 2020 postponed to 2021 due to COVID-19

subtopics.name: Key Olympic Milestones

table.key_fact: First Modern Olympics; 14 nations, 241 athletes; only men participated
Women first allowed to participate; India's first Olympics – Norman Pritchard
Freestyle Wrestling first appeared in Modern Olympics #imp
Last solid gold Olympic medals awarded
Five-ring Olympic flag used for first time ever #imp
First Winter Olympics held
Motto Citius Altius Fortius introduced; India's tennis debut (5 players: 4M + 1F)
India's first Hockey Olympic Gold; Major Dhyan Chand part of winning team
Women's Javelin Throw officially introduced #imp
Olympic Torch Relay tradition starts #imp
India's first Olympics as independent nation; Men's Hockey Gold – Balbir Singh Sr.
K.D. Jadhav – India's first individual medal post-independence (Bronze, Wrestling)
First Winter Olympic Torch Relay
Olympic anthem played first time; Milkha Singh 4th in 400m; Paralympic Games began
First Olympics in Asia; Judo debut; Women's Volleyball introduced; satellite broadcast
India's 8th and final Hockey Gold; Women's Hockey debut; P.T. Usha's Olympic debut
P.T. Usha missed bronze by 0.01 sec in 400m Hurdles
Winter & Summer Olympics separated into alternating 2-year cycle
Centennial Games; Leander Paes – Bronze in Tennis; Mascot: Izzy
Karnam Malleswari – first Indian woman Olympic medallist (Weightlifting)
Abhinav Bindra – India's first individual Olympic Gold (Shooting)
Mixed-team events introduced in Olympics; Sushil Kumar – Silver in Wrestling
Neeraj Chopra – India's first Athletics Olympic Gold (Javelin); Bajrang Punia Bronze
Aman Sehrawat – India's youngest Olympic medallist

exam_tips: Wrestling first included in Ancient Olympics: 708 BCE (Ans: A – Delhi Police Constable)
Olympic torch relay tradition began at: 1936 Berlin (Ans: D – Delhi Police Constable)
Women's Javelin introduced in: 1932 (Ans: D – Delhi Police Constable)
Freestyle wrestling first in Modern Olympics: 1904 St. Louis (Ans: D – Delhi Police Constable)
First Marathon in 1896 route: Marathon to Athens (Ans: C – Delhi Police Constable)

chapters.chapter: Olympic Symbols – Rings, Flag, Flame, Anthem

subtopics.name: Olympic Rings
content: Designed by Pierre de Coubertin in 1913-14
First used at 1920 Antwerp Games #imp
Represent unity of 5 inhabited continents of the world
Background colour: White
Total colours including background: 6 (5 rings + 1 white background)
5 interlocked rings together represent all nations and continents
exam_tips: INCORRECT pairing asked in exam: Black – Antarctica (Ans: C – Delhi Police Constable)
IOC founded in which year = 1894 (Ans: A – Delhi Police Constable)

subtopics.name: Olympic Torch & Flame
content: Flame lit in Olympia, Greece and carried by torch to the host city's cauldron
Torch Relay tradition started at 1936 Berlin Summer Olympics #imp
First Winter Olympic Torch Relay: 1952 Oslo
Symbolizes continuity between ancient and modern games

subtopics.name: Olympic Motto & Anthem
content: Motto: Citius, Altius, Fortius (Swifter, Higher, Stronger) #imp
Motto introduced: 1924 Paris Olympics
Official Olympic Anthem first played at: 1960 Rome Olympics #imp

subtopics.name: Medal Composition

table.composition: 95–97% Copper mixed with Zinc or Tin

content: Last SOLID GOLD Olympic medals awarded: 1912 Stockholm Olympics #imp
Primary metal in Gold medal = Silver (92.5%) – frequently asked MCQ
exam_tips: Which metal primarily used to make Olympic Gold medals = Silver (Ans: B – SSC Phase 13, 02/08/2025)
Which color medal for first position = Gold (Ans: C – SSC Phase 13, 02/08/2025)

subtopics.name: Key Bodies
content: WADA – World Anti-Doping Agency: global body responsible for anti-doping worldwide
NADA – National Anti-Doping Agency: responsible for testing Indian athletes #imp
SAI – Sports Authority of India: NOT anti-doping body

subtopics.name: Penalties for Anabolic Steroids (Olympics)

table.additional: Can be reduced based on degree of fault

table.penalty: Lifetime ban from professional & Olympic sports

table.violation: Refusing drug test or tampering samples

exam_tips: Penalty for using anabolic steroids in Olympics = Disqualification/Lifetime ban (Ans: D – DP HCM 2025, Jan 2026)
Anti-doping agency for Indian athletes = NADA (Ans: B – Delhi Police Constable, Jan 2026)
WADA is responsible for = Anti-doping (Ans: C – Delhi Police Constable, Jan 2026)

subtopics.name: Frequently Asked Sports Terms

table.meaning: Winning all 4 major tennis tournaments in a year
Second chance bracket for athletes who lost to finalists
Removal of athlete's result due to rule violation
Result decided by finish-line photo when athletes finish nearly simultaneously
Events with both male and female athletes in same team; recently introduced
Preliminary rounds to select finalists
Defensive action to deflect or block opponent's attack #imp
Three consecutive achievements (wickets, goals) by one player
Score of zero in tennis
Score tied at 40-40 in tennis; 29-29 in badminton
Serve not touched by opponent in tennis; direct point in volleyball
Hard overhead shot hit downwards
Continuation of bat/club motion after contact
Moving ball with feet/hand while advancing
Line separating the two halves of kabaddi court
Line 3.75m from baulk line – raider must cross to score bonus point
High arc shot over opponent
Position where king is in check and cannot escape – game over
Player not in check but has no legal move – game drawn
Delivery that lands at or near batsman's feet
Off-break delivery by leg-spin bowler
Left-arm wrist-spin delivery that turns from off to leg
Illegal delivery; batting team gets extra run
Ball bowled too wide of stumps; extra run added
Run out of non-striker by bowler before ball delivery – legal but controversial
First goal scored in extra time ends the game immediately (no longer used in FIFA)
When a player scores a goal into their own net
Tie-breaker method – each team takes alternate penalty kicks
Attacker closer to opponent's goal than ball and second-last defender
Opponent floored and unable to rise within 10 seconds
Referee stops fight because one boxer cannot continue
Left-handed stance; left-handed bowler/batsman
Long-distance race of 42.195 km
Middle-distance race with water jump and hurdle barriers
10 events over 2 days for men (100m, 400m, 1500m, 110m hurdles, long jump, high jump, pole vault, shot put, discus, javelin)
7 events over 2 days for women
Combination of cross-country skiing and rifle shooting
A type of jump in figure skating
Technique of going over bar backwards/headfirst
Centre of the target – highest score
Two strokes under par for a hole
One stroke under par for a hole
One stroke over par for a hole

exam_tips: 'Parry' is used in = Fencing (Ans: A – Delhi Police Constable, 29 Dec 2025)

subtopics.name: Indian Olympic Association (IOA)
content: IOA formed: 1927 #imp
Founding President: Sir Dorabji Tata
Founding Secretary: Dr. A.G. Noehren
Headquarters: New Delhi (current)
Role: Apex governing body for Olympic sports in India
Responsibilities: Athlete selection, team management, sending teams to Olympics, Asian Games, CWG
Major events covered: Olympic Games, Asian Games, Commonwealth Games and other international competitions
exam_tips: IOA formed in which year = 1927 (Ans: A – SSC Phase 13, 30/07/2025)
IOA is responsible for = Managing India's Olympic participation (Ans: B – Delhi Police Constable, Jan 2026)

subtopics.name: India's Key Firsts in Olympics

table.name: 1900 Paris Olympics
table.details: Single representative: Norman Pritchard (Anglo-Indian) – 2 Silver medals in 200m sprint & 200m hurdles

table.name: K.D. Jadhav (Khashaba Dadasaheb Jadhav)
table.details: Bronze – Freestyle Wrestling, 1952 Helsinki. Nickname: 'Pocket Dynamo'

table.name: Abhinav Bindra
table.details: Gold – 10m Air Rifle Shooting, 2008 Beijing Olympics #imp

table.name: Hockey
table.details: 8 Golds (1928–1980) – maximum Olympic golds for India

exam_tips: Which Indian shooter won India's first individual Olympic Gold = Abhinav Bindra (Ans: C – SSC CHSL Tier-2, 10 Apr 2026)
First Indian to win Olympic medal after independence = K.D. Jadhav (Ans: B – SSC CPO, 12 Dec 2025)
Sport with most Olympic Golds for India = Hockey (Ans: A – SSC Phase 13, 29/07/2025)
India's single representative at 1900 Paris Olympics = Norman Pritchard (Ans: A)
Indian Men's Hockey team Olympic Gold medals = 8 (Ans: B – SSC CHSL, 27 Nov 2025)

subtopics.name: India's Hockey Olympic Domination (1928–1980)
content: Total Golds: 8 (maximum for India in any sport) #imp
First Hockey Gold: 1928 Amsterdam Olympics
Last Hockey Gold: 1980 Moscow Olympics, Captain: Vasudevan Baskaran
Women's Hockey first qualified: 1980 Moscow (debut), finished 4th

subtopics.name: Dhanraj Pillay
content: Represented India in 4 consecutive Olympics: 1992, 1996, 2000, 2004 #imp
Also played 4 World Cups, 4 Asian Games, 4 Champions Trophies
Absolute cornerstone of Indian Hockey – one of the greatest ever
exam_tips: Dhanraj Pillay played in how many Olympic Games = 4 (Ans: D – Delhi Police Constable, Jan 2026)

chapters.chapter: Key Athletes – Milkha Singh & P.T. Usha

subtopics.name: Milkha Singh – Flying Sikh
content: Nickname: 'Flying Sikh' (also called 'The Flying Sikh of India')
1960 Rome Olympics: Finished 4th in Men's 400m
Timing: 45.6 sec (manual), 45.73 sec (electronic)
FIRST Indian athlete to reach Olympic Athletics Finals #imp
First Indian individual athletics Gold: 1958 Commonwealth Games, Cardiff – 440 yards race
Also won Gold at 1958 Asian Games
National record of 45.6 sec in 400m stood for nearly 2 decades

subtopics.name: P.T. Usha – Golden Girl / Payyoli Express
content: Full name: Pilavullakandi Thekkeparambil Usha
Nicknames: 'Golden Girl', 'Payyoli Express', 'Queen of Track and Field'
Olympic debut: 1980 Moscow, age 16 #imp
1984 Los Angeles Olympics: First Indian woman to reach Olympic Athletics Final
Event: Women's 400m Hurdles
Finished 4th – missed Bronze by just 0.01 seconds (1/100th of a second) #imp
Timing: 55.42 seconds – set national record
1986 Asian Games, Seoul: 4 Gold + 1 Silver
Records set in 200m, 400m, 400m Hurdles, 4×400m Relay at 1986 Asian Games
Awarded Padma Shri in 1985
Her 55.42 sec national record later equalled by Vithya Ramraj at 2022 Hangzhou Asian Games

subtopics.name: Overview
content: World's second largest multi-sport event (after Olympics)
Held every 4 years, only Asian countries participate
Governing body: Olympic Council of Asia (OCA) #imp
Symbol: Bright red sun with 16 rays, underlined by 11 rings
India hosted twice: 1951 and 1982, both in New Delhi
India's best performance: Hangzhou 2023 – 107 medals (28G, 38S, 41B)

subtopics.name: First Asiad – 1951 New Delhi
content: Host city: New Delhi, India
Dates: 4–11 March 1951
Venue: National Stadium, Delhi (now Dhyan Chand National Stadium)
Officially opened by President Rajendra Prasad
Nations: 11 (Afghanistan, Burma, Ceylon, India, Indonesia, Iran, Japan, Nepal, Philippines, Singapore, Thailand)
Athletes: 489
India's first-ever Asian Games Gold: Sachin Nag – Men's 100m Freestyle Swimming #imp
Motto: 'Ever Onward' (from Jawaharlal Nehru's speech) #imp
exam_tips: India's first Asian Games Gold winner = Sachin Nag (Swimming)
1st Asian Games motto = Ever Onward
1st Asian Games opened by = President Rajendra Prasad

subtopics.name: Overview
content: Formerly called British Empire Games (1930–1950)
Now called Commonwealth Games; held every 4 years
Governed by Commonwealth Games Federation (CGF)
India first participated: 1934 London (2nd CWG)
India has hosted CWG twice: 1951 New Delhi (British Empire Games) + 2010 New Delhi (19th CWG) – Note: 1951 is counted as Empire Games
Properly: India first hosted CWG officially in 2010 Delhi

subtopics.name: India's Key CWG Firsts
content: First CWG participation: 1934 London
First CWG athletics individual Gold: Milkha Singh – 440 yards, 1958 Cardiff
First CWG Badminton Gold: Prakash Padukone – Men's Singles, 1978 Edmonton #imp
exam_tips: First CWG Gold for India (individual) = Milkha Singh (Athletics)
First CWG Badminton Gold India = Prakash Padukone

subtopics.name: Delhi 2010 CWG – India's Best Performance
content: Edition: 19th Commonwealth Games
Host: New Delhi, India
Motto: 'Come Out and Play' #imp
India's medal tally: 101 medals (38 Gold, 27 Silver, 36 Bronze) #imp
India finished 2nd in overall medal tally – best ever CWG rank for India #imp
Second time CWG held in Asia (first: 1998 Kuala Lumpur)
Doping case: Rani Yadav (Women's 20 km Race Walk) tested positive for 19-norandrosterone

table.note: India lost Final 2021; India lost Final 2023
Named after Ranjitsinhji; oldest domestic cricket tournament in India
Ranji Trophy champions vs Rest of India
England vs Australia – oldest cricket rivalry

subtopics.name: Key Cricket Facts
content: ICC – International Cricket Council: Global governing body of Cricket, HQ: Dubai, UAE
BCCI – Board of Control for Cricket in India: Richest cricket board in the world
First Cricket World Cup: 1975, England – West Indies won (Captain: Clive Lloyd)
India's first World Cup Gold: 1983 – Captain Kapil Dev; beat West Indies in final
India's 2nd World Cup Gold: 2011 – Captain MS Dhoni; beat Sri Lanka in final (Mumbai)
India won T20 WC 2007: Captain MS Dhoni; beat Pakistan in final
India won T20 WC 2024: Captain Rohit Sharma; beat South Africa in final (Barbados)
Sachin Tendulkar: Most Test runs (15921), Most ODI runs (18426), Most centuries (100 international)
Muttiah Muralitharan: Most Test wickets (800 wickets)
Shane Warne: Most Test wickets by spinner (before Muralitharan)
Fastest century in T20I: Rohit Sharma (35 balls) – against Sri Lanka 2017
First Indian to score 100 in ODI: Kapil Dev (1978)
Lord's Cricket Ground: 'Home of Cricket', London, England

subtopics.name: FIFA World Cup – Key Facts
content: FIFA – Fédération Internationale de Football Association: Global governing body, HQ: Zurich, Switzerland
FIFA World Cup started: 1930, Uruguay – Uruguay won first World Cup
Held every 4 years; most watched sporting event worldwide
Most World Cup wins: Brazil (5 times – 1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, 2002)
Germany & Italy: 4 times each
2022 FIFA World Cup: Qatar – Argentina won (Captain: Lionel Messi)
2026 FIFA World Cup: USA, Canada & Mexico (joint hosts)
Rimet Trophy: Original FIFA World Cup trophy; Jules Rimet – former FIFA president

table.note: Named after Air Marshal Subroto Mukerjee

subtopics.name: Key Football Terms & Rules
content: Team: 11 players per side (including goalkeeper)
Duration: 90 minutes (2 halves of 45 minutes)
Penalty area: 18-yard box around goal
Yellow card: Warning; Red card: Dismissal
Offside trap: Defensive strategy to catch attackers offside
FIFA Laws of the Game: 17 official laws

table.note: Hosted by Malaysia; named after Sultan of Perak
Lifetime achievement award in sports India; named after Major Dhyan Chand

subtopics.name: Major Dhyan Chand – The Wizard
content: Full name: Dhyan Chand Singh
Nickname: 'The Wizard of Hockey'
Born: 29 August 1905 (celebrated as National Sports Day in India) #imp
Olympic Golds: 1928 Amsterdam, 1932 Los Angeles, 1936 Berlin
Scored 570 goals in international career
Hitler offered him German citizenship and high military position – he declined
Bharat Ratna: Government recommended in 2014 but not awarded yet
National Sports Day: 29 August (Dhyan Chand's birthday)

subtopics.name: Balbir Singh Sr.
content: Full name: Balbir Singh Dosanjh
Three Olympic Golds: 1948 London, 1952 Helsinki, 1956 Melbourne
Top scorer at 1952 Helsinki Olympics
In 1952, scored 9 goals in finals (world record for long time)
Also managed/coached Indian team to Gold at 1975 Hockey World Cup

subtopics.name: FIH & Hockey Rules
content: FIH – Fédération Internationale de Hockey: Global governing body, HQ: Lausanne, Switzerland
Team: 11 players on field per side
Duration: 4 quarters of 15 minutes (60 minutes total)
Penalty corner: Also called short corner
Penalty stroke: 1-on-1 with goalkeeper from penalty spot
Green card: 2-minute suspension; Yellow card: 5-10 min suspension; Red card: permanent exclusion

table.note: Oldest and most prestigious badminton tournament; held since 1899
BWF Men's World Team Championship; India won 2022
Held annually; India's P.V. Sindhu won 2019
International badminton tournament held in Lucknow

table.notable: First Indian CWG Badminton Gold (1978 Edmonton); All England 1980; Arjuna Award
2016 Rio Silver (first Indian woman Olympic Silver in Badminton); 2021 Tokyo Bronze; BWF World Champion 2019; Padma Bhushan
2012 London Bronze (first Indian badminton Olympic medal); World No. 1 ranking; Padma Bhushan
BWF World Championship Silver 2021; multiple Super Series titles
All England Champion 2001; Chief National Coach; Dronacharya Award

subtopics.name: Badminton Rules & Terms
content: BWF – Badminton World Federation: Global governing body, HQ: Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia
Game: Best of 3 sets, each set to 21 points; deuce at 29-29 (next point wins)
Court dimensions: Singles – 13.4m × 5.18m; Doubles – 13.4m × 6.1m
Net height: 1.55m at posts, 1.524m at centre
Shuttle cock: 16 feathers; weight: 4.74–5.50 grams

subtopics.name: Chess Governing Bodies & Championships
content: FIDE – Fédération Internationale des Échecs: Global governing body of Chess, HQ: Lausanne, Switzerland
Title of World Chess Champion awarded by FIDE
First official World Chess Champion: Wilhelm Steinitz (1886)
World Chess Champion 2023: Ding Liren (China – beat Nepomniachtchi)
World Chess Champion 2024: Gukesh D (India) – became youngest ever World Chess Champion at age 18 #imp
Gukesh D beat Ding Liren in November 2024 to win title
Viswanathan Anand: India's greatest chess player; World Champion 5 times (2000, 2007, 2008, 2010, 2012)

subtopics.name: Indian Chess Grandmasters (Key Names)
content: Viswanathan Anand – First Indian chess Grandmaster (1988); 5 World titles
Gukesh D – Youngest World Chess Champion (2024); age 18
Praggnanandhaa R – Youngest Grandmaster at 12 years, 10 months (2018)
Koneru Humpy – Women's World Rapid Chess Champion
D. Harika – Multiple Women's World Championship medals

subtopics.name: Rajiv Gandhi Khel Ratna (Major Dhyan Chand Khel Ratna Award)
content: Highest sports honour in India #imp
Renamed from 'Rajiv Gandhi Khel Ratna' to 'Major Dhyan Chand Khel Ratna Award' in 2021
Award: Rs 25 lakh cash prize + trophy + scroll
First recipient: Viswanathan Anand (Chess, 1991–92) #imp
Notable recipients: Sachin Tendulkar, PV Sindhu, Virat Kohli, Neeraj Chopra, Bajrang Punia, Aman Sehrawat

subtopics.name: Arjuna Award
content: Second most prestigious national sports award in India
Instituted in: 1961 #imp
Awarded for: Consistent outstanding performance over 4 years
Award: Rs 15 lakh cash + bronze statue of Arjuna + scroll
First recipients (1961): Multiple players across sports – Salim Durani (Cricket), Gurbachan Singh Randhawa (Athletics), etc.

subtopics.name: Dronacharya Award
content: Highest award for sports coaches in India
Instituted in: 1985 #imp
Award: Statuette of Dronacharya + Rs 15 lakh + certificate
Named after: Drona (Dronacharya) – legendary teacher from Mahabharata
First recipients (1985): Om Prakash Bhardwaj (Boxing), Bhalachandra Bhaskaran Bhaskar (Athletics), O.M. Nambiar (Athletics)

subtopics.name: Dhyan Chand Award
content: Lifetime Achievement Award in Sports in India
Instituted in: 2002
Given to: Athletes who contributed to sports even after retirement
Named after: Major Dhyan Chand – legendary Indian hockey player

subtopics.name: Rashtriya Khel Protsahan Puraskar
content: Given to: Corporate entities and individuals who promote sports
Instituted in: 2009

chapters.chapter: Key Sports Venues & Stadiums in India

table.note: Named after Major Dhyan Chand; 1951 Asian Games venue
Home of Cricket; MCC (Marylebone Cricket Club)

table.capacity: 100,024 (largest cricket stadium globally before Narendra Modi)
